So glad to be out of the hospital after the first round of it. And I got on the treadmill. I was doing this because I was doing indoors. Yes, or whatever. And I fell off. I could't understand why I fell off. I actually have, what's called neuropathy in my feet.
And my, I now have no two steps are exactly the same, and so I can't really keep a rhythm. That's running stories, but I think Demetrius has probably much better running stories. But really the question detrius is like as you think about like the way that you manage as a distance runner.
Like what goes on in your head right when you are, you know, in that middle point like the are there ideas there are there pans there that like li can be putting to work to be getting through like this messy middle as well.

Yeah. That's when I explained the same thing to my own other health where she has a lot of frustrations in her mental health because of the pressure of her finances because of the new beginning with the pharmacy. She stopped being employed with the security of all of these umbrellas and all these positive stuff and suddenly got upfront of running a business taking care of the things from zero to 100. Everything is under her hands.
She realized that if you want to make money, you have to go outside to the market where an employee is something that never can see that easily. Even if she was able to see that, she wasn't able to realize how painful it can be.
So we have this almost every day, trying to encourage her because at the same time, she was me being so cool and nice. I track their bank accounts. I see them dropping down. I explain here that I took a big risk because one almost one year where I was working mostly with make consulting, building automations, and air table consulting, building stuff in there. It was quite nice, but I decided to switch to a totally different domain in doing my next step.
I explain here that I took the risk to do it at the point where, as a family, me and her, we were in a big financial risk. Because if I had to remain back in where I was, I may have had the security of my own umbrella, but I would lose the security of a bigger horizon.
I'm very glad I joined in a greater horizon. Where I am more free and without limits on where I want to go in this practice, and comparing myself with other air table consultants who have no clue what's going to happen after structuring the data. How can we use them? Which is part of my journey so far. I'm very glad that I'm there.
I faced the difficulties of working for my personal branding communication, recognizing all of those skills I'm not having and how great consultants can help me and push me to my own limits and improve myself.
So all of this journey was an improvement. Now, what I say to her is that when we hit the wall, which is the point Ray has just described, is that point where you feel so disappointed because you don't see any alarming of success. You have so much pain. You feel so tired, you lose your actual mental health at the same time.
Okay, so you have some concerns about why you get started. The thing is not why you get started is the act question is why we have to continue. We continue not only because of the journey, but because of what we expect to see at the finish line. You don't think of the marathon like a flat race. Think about it as a mountain run. Everybody starts to run to go on hiking or even have a walk. Not because of the journey, but mostly for the view at the peak.
This is the most encouraging thing. I'm not trying to do all my best. I have three months almost now working almost every single day, twelve hours a day because this is the peak I want to go. I'm not running so fast in this journey, but I'm working, making things done so I can push week by week myself to the big now.
It's not easy to listen to these romantic words. It sounds like poetry, which actually doesn't make any sense. The truth is, think up again where you were. Those dark moments where you don't see an alarm. Compare yourself to where you were when you made the decision to switch. On the day of National Women Day, I had a post about who is the woman who changed my life. Excess of my people and my family and all of this kind of person.
You changed the way I'm making decisions. She said something important. "Don't criticize your decisions based on the outcome. Criticize them based on all the facts you have at the moment of making that decision."
Okay. So are you in a better position than where you are? Nice, keep it, are you learning? Yes, all right. The thing that you are in a painful position means that something doesn't pay you back yet. Okay, let's work on that. Let's talk about these things. Let's recognize even the recognition of the things that don't work.
It's a huge step. Even in an example of a marathon, if you realize that you have a pain and a muscle, you have to do some stretching and you can move. You have a stomach disease. Stop, drink some water and then continue. Recognize what's going on there.
Of course, we're being a member of a community with valuable people. I find it to be the greatest investment overall because not only can you receive supportive words from people, but you can receive nice ideas from people having similar journeys. How many incredible ideas are in these sessions?
From you, from Robert, from Ray, too many learnings. Even one piece of them, if we put them down, we make bigger progress. That makes sense. You guys say, "I was so weatic so philosophica."

ideas.text: Copyrighted material being subjected to DMCA claims indicates its authenticity and value.
ideas.sourceQuote: It's getting DMCA all over the place because it's actually copyrighted stuff. So it's actually real. That's how, you know, it's real.

ideas.text: Examining how a system like Claude sets up its system prompt can offer insights into its operational design.
ideas.sourceQuote: it's actually probably interesting for looking at, like, how it thinks about setting up the, system prompt and all that.

ideas.text: The information security landscape in the AI space is rapidly evolving, marked by significant vulnerabilities such as source code exposure and supply chain attacks.
ideas.sourceQuote: This is the fast-moving world of information security right now in the AI space.

ideas.text: AI is likely being used to generate and disseminate malware in current supply chain attacks.
ideas.sourceQuote: Presumably they're using AI to generate these things and pop and populate them.

ideas.text: Working with a structured thinker consultant can provide invaluable insights and prevent significant wasted effort in new projects.
ideas.sourceQuote: he's given me insights and understanding that I just. I either wouldn't have got or would have just taken like four weeks of doing everything wrong first.

ideas.text: A structured approach to knowledge graph building involves central ingestion rules, logging raw data in one place, and processing data elsewhere, enabling self-improvement.
ideas.sourceQuote: He helped me, build a structure around this project that wasn't there before. So I have, like. I have to. I have central rules that always must be observed through ingesting.

ideas.text: Noting every decision made helps prevent forgetting and provides a reliable reference point for project review and error checking.
ideas.sourceQuote: The other thing I've got it start doing is noting every decision that I make. Because what I've found a lot of the time is that I forget what decisions I've made because you're making them so fast.

ideas.text: The current mode of working can be exhausting, feeling both managerial and micromanaging due to rapid decision-making across multiple chat windows.
ideas.sourceQuote: So it's almost like it's much more sort of managerial but micromanaging at the same time on some levels. So it's quite tiring.

ideas.text: The scope of data ingestion for the knowledge graph is vast, incorporating scraping, APIs, and direct data file downloads.
ideas.sourceQuote: In terms of the scope of getting it. So, some of it's scraping, some of it's apiis, some of it's just downloading massive data files, but it's been a big old learning thing.

ideas.text: The knowledge graph can generate valuable political insights, including policy comparisons with a universal framework, identifying key constituencies, and mapping donor ideological alignment.
ideas.sourceQuote: I'm able to say to them, These electors, these constituencies, are the ones that are right for you. This is why. By the way, this donor gives to this think tank and this party and this think tank, and he's spoken at this event. All of these are ideologically in your wheelhouse, and all of this stuff is starting.

ideas.text: Clients often value understanding the project approach, thought process, and clear outcomes (what's done and what's still missing) more than absolute perfection by a deadline.
ideas.sourceQuote: they don't care about the perfection. They mostly care about the approach, the way I was thinking during the process, and at the end, they care about what I've already done.

ideas.text: Recognizing and admitting missing elements or uncertainties in a project is a valid reason to request more time, as it indicates a thorough and effective process.
ideas.sourceQuote: So I recognize that I'm missing or I'm not totally sure for these things. So I need some more time, which is cool to ask for more time, because you found something that made the time, not less effective.

ideas.text: Prioritizing mental health by avoiding unnecessary stress from client expectations or project demands is crucial, and additional work should be compensated.
ideas.sourceQuote: Ideally, I don't want to keep on my back the stress of other people, okay? If they want me to do that, they have to pay even more because the deal was to do that thing. Come on, guys. Yeah, it's about mental health. Why should I get stressed because of you?

ideas.text: A robust system that captures project details can serve as a mental 'reset' button when a project feels overwhelming or disorganized.
ideas.sourceQuote: If at any point, I'm like, God, I just don't know where everything is and everything's a mess. I can just go tell me this and it just starts me again.

ideas.text: To make the 'messy middle' of a project less painful, identify the specific points of friction and work towards making those processes more efficient.
ideas.sourceQuote: what is the messy middle part of the process that you're mentioning and can it just not be so painful anymore? Just in terms of, right like as a business right like or as a process for something that you're just trying to make more efficient.

ideas.text: Leveraging available technology can provide clarity and accelerate progress, even when motivation is low or under pressure.
ideas.sourceQuote: there's a lot of technology that can be deployed to be able to gain more clarity on things if or be able to gain leverage on like to get stuff done even if motivation is necessarily maybe not as high or if there's pressures and.

ideas.text: Building robust systems, especially automated ones, is crucial for ensuring consistency and providing reliable support during challenging project phases.
ideas.sourceQuote: I'm putting energy into just making them more capable. Building up. Just their ability. To influence certain things in a certain way. And for those times, right, like for consistency that might that comes out of just putting a machine towards it, I think.

ideas.text: Small automated tasks, like meeting summaries, contribute to business consistency, reduce mental load, and cumulatively improve engagement.
ideas.sourceQuote: Even just the thing that... Even just like an email, meeting summary that goes out automatically, for example, it's just like that. It's a small thing that's off my mind. That is just a bit of consistency too for the business that... It adds up.

ideas.text: Visual representations of work, such as whiteboards, are particularly valuable for making 'invisible' digital or technical progress manifest and understandable to clients.
ideas.sourceQuote: It's like making manifest. And it's actually kind of the point of it too, was actually for that to allow the client to see that there was stuff like the scale of what's being done and to be able to just, yeah, encapsulate, especially when you're using the technology and you're like going so quickly and just to be able to like keep up with that. But yes, exactly, because a lot of this stuff can be very invisible.

ideas.text: Creating bespoke frontends is an increasingly accessible and cost-effective way to visualize complex data and provide an additional dimension to a project.
ideas.sourceQuote: I've found a lot of utility in creating, like Fronttent that are bespoke for, like, specific. It's, you know, cheaper than ever. It doesn't take too much to be able to create a front end, obviously.

ideas.text: Data analysts frequently face the challenge of deriving meaningful conclusions from vast datasets, a process that lacks a standardized operational procedure.
ideas.sourceQuote: every data analyst faces where they have tons of data front and they are responsible to recognize and get meaningful results and outcomes conclusions just looking at the data. So this is not a standard operational procedure.

ideas.text: When navigating the 'messy middle' of a project, shortening the perceived 'swim' (breaking it into smaller parts) or increasing visibility of progress are key strategies.
ideas.sourceQuote: I think there are two things you can do about that, right? One is to just shorten the view. Right? Shortened swim. We talk about shortened sharp like what are those bits in the middle. Second, is just to be more aware of where we are in the progress, right?

ideas.text: Tools that enhance visibility and allow interrogation of progress during a project can clarify how far one has come and how much further there is to go, potentially shortening the overall effort.
ideas.sourceQuote: The kinds of things we're talking about and the tools that are available to us allow you to see a lot more clearly and to be interrogating your progress, which then makes it clear how far you've come and it gives you a better idea of how far it is to go. Actually, on the swim, you can actually potentially shorten the swim through that greater handle on that information, right?

ideas.text: Maintaining comprehension of the project's direction as progress is made, rather than just experiencing a decline in energy, is crucial for managing the 'messy middle.'
ideas.sourceQuote: Basically, your comprehension is increasing as you go forward, right? As opposed to just your energy level falling as you go forward.

ideas.text: The adoption of AI technologies has fundamentally altered working methods, making them more comfortable and efficient due to technological improvements, but also requiring continuous learning.
ideas.sourceQuote: I know you two have been working at all. You three have been working on a much more detailed basis with AI for a lot longer. What have you noticed that's changed in the way you work over the last year or two? Significantly.

ideas.text: Activating 'dangerous allow all permissions' in software can streamline workflow by eliminating constant prompts, though it entails security risks.
ideas.sourceQuote: I turned on the 'dangerous allow all permissions' thing or whatever. So you're not constantly going, Yes, carry on, right? And it's like, Good, that's done. Because I was really annoyed by it.

ideas.text: Subscribing to premium AI services (e.g., Claude Max) removes usage and context limitations, enhancing productivity and creative flow.
ideas.sourceQuote: You get the Claude Max subscription, so you're not worrying about running out of context and usage and stuff like that. Great.

ideas.text: Breaking down tasks into small, time-boxed increments (e.g., less than an hour) and using an accountability partner enhances project visibility and helps manage the 'messy middle.'
ideas.sourceQuote: Every morning I meet with my accountability partner or whatever, and I'd say, I'm going to do these things, and I estimate how long I think something will take, and I try to break it down so that nothing takes more than an hour.

ideas.text: Accurately predicting task durations in new or AI-driven projects is challenging due to the unpredictable nature of emerging tools and processes.
ideas.sourceQuote: I can't predict this stuff because it's so... It's all a new world on some level, and I'm having to learn.

ideas.text: The use of multiple contact windows and contexts simultaneously has become more comfortable with improved technology and familiarity with AI tools.
ideas.sourceQuote: I'm working with these things at like five windows at a time a lot of the time. And for me, I'm pretty comfortable with holding, like now multiple contacts at the same time and just being able to switch between them. I've well, the technology got a lot better, so it's a lot more comfortable to use the tools as well as.

ideas.text: Breaking down tasks into smaller, hourly segments helps avoid a 'messy middle' and increases visibility into project progress.
ideas.sourceQuote: I estimate how long I think something will take, and I try to break it down so that nothing takes more than an hour. So that I'm not just going through something that has a big, messy middle. That visibility thing you're saying.

ideas.text: Predicting task duration in new and complex domains is difficult due to the constant learning involved.
ideas.sourceQuote: I can't predict this stuff because it's so... It's all a new world on some level, and I'm having to learn.

ideas.text: External structures and support systems, like a consultant or supportive colleagues, are crucial for creating scaffolding and visibility to navigate challenging phases and avoid the 'messy middle'.
ideas.sourceQuote: I have enough because there are these structures in place, including Demetrius. Thank you for being a structure that just helps create the scaffolding around it for that visibility and even just people on the sales going, 'Keep swimming. It's okay.'

ideas.text: Working with AI often involves managing multiple context windows simultaneously.
ideas.sourceQuote: I'm working with these things at like five windows at a time a lot of the time.

ideas.text: Improved AI technology makes using these tools more comfortable and efficient.
ideas.sourceQuote: The technology got a lot better, so it's a lot more comfortable to use the tools as well as.

ideas.text: Gaining more knowledge about AI tools and optimizing personal workflows is key to effective usage and self-improvement of the AI.
ideas.sourceQuote: Just having more knowledge about how to use them and get them to improve themselves and to figure out, you know, a workflow that works for me.

ideas.text: AI reduces friction for experimentation, allowing users to try new things and quickly assess their viability.
ideas.sourceQuote: Having less friction to like, basically. No friction to trying new things. Like just, you know, trying to try something very fast and finding out if it's not, you know, if it's going to work or not very quickly.

ideas.text: AI enables faster iteration through the entire lifecycle of solutions, increasing the volume of development work.
ideas.sourceQuote: Just basically trying to see things through from beginning to end of a total life cycle of, you know, solutions like just as much as possible like to get as many reps of that in as much as possible. That's another thing I think that I. Yeah, like that's just by nature of it, just because you're able to just get there so much quicker.

ideas.text: Using multiple-choice prompts with AI can help clarify user intent and reveal insights by showing what the user doesn't want.
ideas.sourceQuote: Give you multiple choice. And then that way I can just go choose one of them. Or usually none of them are good, but, like, it tells me what I. But it tells me what I don't want. And then that way, like, you know, pretty much makes it clear what I do want when I read that stuff.

ideas.text: Switching between multiple AI context windows, even for the same job, is a new type of cognitive load that can be tiring, akin to working a new muscle.
ideas.sourceQuote: It is interesting. I'm feeling tired, but it's like a new muscle that's been worked switching between multiple different context windows and still... But it's the same job, so you're switching the subject context, but the job itself is the same thing. So it's like a different kind of context switching, I think.

ideas.text: The feeling of 'hitting the wall' in a challenging journey is characterized by deep disappointment, pain, fatigue, and mental health strain due to a perceived lack of success.
ideas.sourceQuote: When we hit the wall... is that point where you feel so disappointed because you don't see any alarming of success. You have so much pain. You feel so tired, you lose your actual mental health at the same time.

ideas.text: The motivation to persevere through difficult challenges comes primarily from the anticipated outcome at the 'finish line' or 'peak', rather than solely from the initial reason for starting.
ideas.sourceQuote: The thing is not why you get started is the act question is why we have to continue. We continue not only because of the journey, but because of what we expect to see at the finish line.

ideas.text: Consistent, steady effort and 'making things done' week by week are more crucial for progress towards a long-term goal than striving for speed.
ideas.sourceQuote: I'm not running so fast in this journey, but I'm working, making things done so I can push week by week myself to the big now.

ideas.text: Acknowledging skill gaps and seeking help from great consultants is vital for personal branding, self-improvement, and pushing personal limits.
ideas.sourceQuote: I faced the difficulties of working for my personal branding communication, recognizing all of those skills I'm not having and how great consultants can help me and push me to my own limits and improve myself.

ideas.text: Evaluating decisions should be based on the facts available at the moment the decision was made, rather than criticizing them solely on their outcomes.
ideas.sourceQuote: Don't criticize your decisions based on the outcome. Criticize them based on all the facts you have at the moment of making that decision.

ideas.text: Recognizing what isn't working and learning from painful experiences is a significant step forward.
ideas.sourceQuote: The thing that you are in a painful position means that something doesn't pay you back yet. Okay, let's work on that. Let's talk about these things. Let's recognize even the recognition of the things that don't work. It's a huge step.

ideas.text: Self-awareness and addressing specific issues (like physical pain during a marathon) are essential for continuing and overcoming challenges.
ideas.sourceQuote: Even in an example of a marathon, if you realize that you have a pain and a muscle, you have to do some stretching and you can move. You have a stomach disease. Stop, drink some water and then continue. Recognize what's going on there.

ideas.text: Being part of a valuable community is a significant investment for receiving both emotional support and practical ideas from people with similar journeys.
ideas.sourceQuote: Of course, we're being a member of a community with valuable people. I find it to be the greatest investment overall because not only can you receive supportive words from people, but you can receive nice ideas from people having similar journeys.

ideas.text: The fear of regret or not finishing a significant undertaking can be a powerful motivator.
ideas.sourceQuote: partly what pushed me through, I think, was how bad I'd feel if I didn't finish it as well. Like how gutted I would feel after all that energy if I just didn't finish.

ideas.text: Focusing on the positive future outcome or 'view at the peak' is a more effective motivator than focusing on the negative feelings of potential failure.
ideas.sourceQuote: I think partly I need to think about what the view will be like when I finish as well, rather than just... I feel like crap if I don't do that.

ideas.text: In moments of fatigue or 'hitting the wall', identifying and focusing intensely on a core element (like 'loving the ball') can instinctively guide actions towards success without overthinking.
ideas.sourceQuote: So there's one thought that you can hold in your head. Let's love the ball because if you love the ball, you'll move towards it, and if you love the ball, you'll pay attention to it. If you're paying attention to it and you're moving towards it, you are far more likely to be successful than if you don't do those things.

ideas.text: A supportive group or community can help individuals navigate challenging periods by shortening the path, providing clarity, or offering elevation, preventing them from being stuck in 'the mire'.
ideas.sourceQuote: what can we do? You know, both yourself, but putting, like, this group together to work for you, right? To help elevate you from it. So you're not just in the mire. So we can find ways to be shortening the path or shining light on it or whatever, right?

concepts.name: Messy Middle
concepts.description: The challenging phase of a project where one is too far from the initial excitement of starting and too far from the final sprint to completion, often characterized by uncertainty, flagging motivation, and a lack of clear progress markers.
concepts.sourceQuote: when you're in that messy middle of a project. Have you got any processes, rituals, mindset stuff? Anything whatsoever you do to help you push through the bit where you're too far from the beginning?

concepts.name: Policy Mapping Framework
concepts.description: A structured analytical approach to compare and contrast policies from different entities (e.g., political parties) using a consistent, universal frame, designed to overcome the inherent philosophical differences in how policies are presented.
concepts.sourceQuote: I've created a policy mapping sort of framework so that you can compare and contrast policy between different parties without it having or with a universal frame because it's quite difficult to compare because everyone obviously has their own sort of political philosophical outlook on things.

concepts.name: The Messy Middle
concepts.description: The challenging, sustained period of effort in a project or journey that occurs between the initial start and before the finish line is in sight, where most of the significant work takes place.
concepts.sourceQuote: this middle part, which is where a lot of the work is.

concepts.name: Mountain Run Metaphor
concepts.description: A mental model for approaching long-term challenges, suggesting that like a mountain run where hikers are motivated by the 'view at the peak' (the end goal), one should focus on the anticipated outcome rather than just the journey itself.
concepts.sourceQuote: You don't think of the marathon like a flat race. Think about it as a mountain run. Everybody starts to run to go on hiking or even have a walk. Not because of the journey, but mostly for the view at the peak.

concepts.name: Decision-making Principle (Outcome vs. Context)
concepts.description: A principle for evaluating past decisions by focusing on the information and facts available at the moment the decision was made, rather than judging them solely based on their eventual outcomes.
concepts.sourceQuote: Don't criticize your decisions based on the outcome. Criticize them based on all the facts you have at the moment of making that decision.

concepts.name: Begin with the End in Mind
concepts.description: A principle, likely from Stephen Covey's 'The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People,' which emphasizes starting any endeavor with a clear understanding of your destination or desired outcome.
concepts.sourceQuote: One of the seven principles that Ray just dropped in there. Begin with the end in mind and habits.

people.name: Robert
people.context: Participating in the discussion, sharing insights on client management, seeking new business, and using technology for automation.

people.name: Ray
people.context: Host of the session, discussing AI security issues and facilitating the conversation, asking Ellie about her project challenges.

people.name: Ellie Rofe
people.context: Client of Demetrius, building a knowledge graph for strategic communications, facing challenges in project management, and preparing for a high-stakes political presentation.

people.name: Demetrius
people.context: A consultant working with Ellie Rofe, providing structural insights for her knowledge graph project, and working on communication and marketing for his own product, Raingraph.

people.name: Bennois
people.context: A contact of Robert's, described as an 'interesting source' and a 'positive person' through whom good things often happen.

people.name: Alex Hormosi
people.context: An author whose book provides advice on structuring and pricing events, specifically for small groups.

people.name: Party Leader
people.context: An unnamed political figure Ellie Rofe is scheduled to meet for dinner to present early results from her knowledge graph project.

people.name: Party Chairman
people.context: An unnamed political figure Ellie Rofe is scheduled to meet for dinner alongside the Party Leader.

people.name: Ellie's other half
people.context: Mentioned by Ellie as someone who tangibly built kitchen cabinets, contrasting with her less visible digital work.

people.name: Demetrius's wife
people.context: Started a new pharmacy business, facing financial and operational pressures after leaving secure employment.

people.name: CEO
people.context: Sponsored Ellie to run a marathon in a Wonder Woman costume, described as 'a bit skeey'.

organisations.name: Anthropic
organisations.context: An AI company whose coding platform, Claude, had its source code accidentally exposed.

organisations.name: NPM
organisations.context: Responsible for managing widely used software packages; they rolled back a malicious version after a supply chain attack.

organisations.name: Zapier
organisations.context: A platform for which Demetrius is undergoing a verification process to connect with Brain Graph.

organisations.name: Raingraph
organisations.context: Demetrius's project or product for which he is developing use cases and focusing on communication and marketing.

organisations.name: East India Club
organisations.context: An exclusive club on Pall Mall in London where Ellie Rofe is scheduled to meet the party leader and chairman for dinner.

stories.title: Anthropic Claude Source Code Exposure
stories.narrative: A recent 'scandal' where the entire source code for Anthropic's popular Claude coding platform was accidentally exposed for half a minute before being quickly acquired by others, highlighting a significant information security incident.
stories.hook: The entire source code. I was suddenly exposed for half a minute and then grabbed by a bunch of folks.
stories.lesson: Even leading AI companies face significant information security risks, including accidental source code exposure.

stories.title: Software Supply Chain Attack via Malicious Package
stories.narrative: A recent incident where a widely used software package was infiltrated with malware designed to steal user data. A new, compromised version was uploaded, but the issue was detected, and NPM quickly rolled back to the previous, secure version.
stories.hook: Earlier this morning there was a big... What's called a supply chain attack. So a relatively popular, widely used package... Was infiltrated, so that the people... The new version went up that was... That contained malware, basically that would steal your stuff.
stories.lesson: Supply chain attacks are a significant and fast-moving threat in the software ecosystem, requiring vigilant monitoring and rapid response.

stories.title: Demetrius's Scraper Results Presentation
stories.narrative: Demetrius recounted a recent experience where he presented scraper results to Ellie. During the presentation, he realized a couple of data points were missing. Instead of hiding it, he focused on explaining his process, workflow, where it failed, and why, then investigated and delivered the complete results hours later. This demonstrated his approach to client communication and problem-solving.
stories.hook: you experienced it a few days ago when I came up to show you the scraper results. I expected that the scraper results were done. We had all the data inside. But when I got in, I realized that a couple of data were missing.
stories.lesson: Transparency about process and challenges, even with imperfections, builds client trust and understanding, and allows for effective problem-solving.

stories.title: Ellie's Awkward Political Dinner
stories.narrative: Ellie describes her anxieties about an upcoming dinner meeting with a party leader and chairman at the East India Club in London. Despite her confidence in the powerful insights her knowledge graph will provide, she feels nervous about the social aspects, like knowing what to wear and navigating dinner etiquette, contrasting her excitement for the work with her personal discomfort.
stories.hook: next Wednesday, which I'm slightly nervous about. So this is ridiculous. But. So next Wednesday, I'm. I'm going to meet with the party leader and the party chairman in the East India Club on Pall Mall in London, which is about as ridiculous as it sounds.
stories.lesson: Even when confident in professional work, social anxieties can still arise in high-stakes personal interactions. It highlights the contrast between analytical confidence and social performance.

stories.title: Robert's Automated 'Dev Update' Skill
stories.narrative: Robert created an automated skill that, upon a simple voice command ('dev update'), reviews the project, documents the gap since the last update, generates a whiteboard image of the progress, and stores it in a specific, standardized location. This automation eliminated a previously painful and time-consuming manual client communication task, allowing him to focus on other problems.
stories.hook: I set up a skill that basically, when I say dev update, it'll go... It'll just go through the project and then look at the previous update and then document the gap. Create a whiteboard image and put it into a specific location every time, like in a very specific standard.
stories.lesson: Automating tedious but essential communication tasks can significantly reduce project pain points and free up mental resources for core problem-solving.

stories.title: Kitchen Cabinets vs. Invisible Code
stories.narrative: Ellie contrasts her partner's tangible accomplishment of building kitchen cabinets from scratch in a day with her own experience of spending ten hours working with chat windows, which results in 'lines of code somewhere' that are not immediately visible. This highlights the challenge of making abstract digital work manifest and its scale understandable.
stories.hook: So my other half, you know, on Friday, he built some kitchen cabinets from scratch. And like at the end of the day, there are some kitchen cabinets there, and I spent ten hours flitting between different chat windows. At the end of the day, you just go, There's a lot of stuff I can't see. It's great, but there's a lot of stuff that's just lines of code somewhere.
stories.lesson: The tangible nature of physical creation makes progress immediately visible, whereas digital work often requires deliberate methods (like whiteboards) to demonstrate scale and impact.

stories.title: Ray's Running Journey Interrupted by Neuropathy
stories.narrative: Ray attempted to take up running before being diagnosed with cancer. During his recovery, he tried running on a treadmill but fell off due to neuropathy in his feet, which prevents him from keeping a consistent rhythm as no two steps are exactly the same.
stories.lesson: Physical limitations and health challenges can significantly impact personal pursuits like running, even when motivated.

stories.title: Demetrius's Wife's Pharmacy Business Struggles
stories.narrative: Demetrius's wife transitioned from secure employment to starting her own pharmacy business. She faced significant financial pressure and frustration, realizing the immense pain and effort required to build a business from scratch and engage with the market, something not evident as an employee.
stories.hook: The painful reality of starting a business after leaving secure employment.
stories.lesson: The transition from employment security to entrepreneurship can be financially and mentally challenging, requiring direct engagement with the market.

stories.title: Demetrius's Risky Career Switch for Greater Horizon
stories.narrative: Demetrius, after working for about a year in make consulting and AirTable consulting, decided to switch to a totally different domain. This was a significant financial risk for his family, but he sought the security of a 'greater horizon' where he would have more freedom and fewer limits, leveraging his unique skills beyond just data structuring.
stories.hook: Taking a family financial risk for the promise of a greater professional horizon.
stories.lesson: Strategic career changes, though risky, can lead to greater freedom and opportunity when driven by a desire for a wider impact and leveraging unique skills.

stories.title: Ellie's Wonder Woman Marathon
stories.narrative: Many years ago, Ellie ran a marathon sponsored by a 'skeey' CEO, wearing a Wonder Woman costume that was too tight. Around mile 19, she developed a terrible stitch and only found relief and could continue by adjusting the costume to allow more breathing room. Her motivation to finish was largely driven by the fear of how 'gutted' she would feel if she didn't complete it after all the effort.
stories.hook: The unexpected challenge of an ill-fitting costume during a marathon.
stories.lesson: Unexpected external factors can hinder performance, and self-awareness (physical pain) and a strong internal motivation (avoiding regret) are crucial for perseverance.

techniques.name: Decision Logging
techniques.description: A personal or project management practice where every significant decision made is recorded to prevent forgetting, provide a historical record, and serve as a reference for future review or troubleshooting.
techniques.sourceQuote: The other thing I've got it start doing is noting every decision that I make. Because what I've found a lot of the time is that I forget what decisions I've made because you're making them so fast.

techniques.name: Transparent Process Communication
techniques.description: A client communication strategy that involves openly discussing the project's approach, workflow, and even where it might fail or what's missing, rather than solely focusing on a perfectly completed end-product. This fosters trust and allows for collaborative problem-solving.
techniques.sourceQuote: But when I got in, I realized that a couple of data were missing. So wow, what happened here? Okay, but I focused my presentation for these couple of minutes. You saw how I worked on that. The process, the workflow, where it fails, and why it may fail.

techniques.name: Messy Middle Pain Point Optimization
techniques.description: An approach to project management that involves identifying the most painful or inefficient aspects within the 'messy middle' phase of a project and actively seeking ways to streamline or automate them to reduce friction and improve overall efficiency.
techniques.sourceQuote: what is the messy middle part of the process that you're mentioning and can it just not be so painful anymore? Just in terms of, right like as a business right like or as a process for something that you're just trying to make more efficient.

techniques.name: Automated Client Communication (e.g., Email Summaries)
techniques.description: The practice of setting up automated systems to handle routine client communications, such as sending meeting summaries, to ensure consistency, reduce manual effort, and free up mental capacity.
techniques.sourceQuote: Even just the thing that... Even just like an email, meeting summary that goes out automatically, for example, it's just like that. It's a small thing that's off my mind.

techniques.name: Automated Project Update Skill (Robert's Dev Update)
techniques.description: A custom automation that responds to a verbal command (e.g., 'dev update') by reviewing project progress, documenting the gap since the last update, generating a visual representation (like a whiteboard image), and storing it in a standardized location for easy client communication without manual intervention.
techniques.sourceQuote: I set up a skill that basically, when I say dev update, it'll go... It'll just go through the project and then look at the previous update and then document the gap. Create a whiteboard image and put it into a specific location every time, like in a very specific standard.

techniques.name: Bespoke Frontend Creation
techniques.description: The process of developing simple, customized user interfaces (frontends) for specific purposes, often to visualize and interact with complex backend data or systems, making them more accessible and understandable.
techniques.sourceQuote: I've found a lot of utility in creating, like Fronttent that are bespoke for, like, specific. It's, you know, cheaper than ever. It doesn't take too much to be able to create a front end, obviously. And like, that's another way to, you know, bring something to the front, right?

techniques.name: Stakeholder 'Soul' Generation for Presentations
techniques.description: A proposed method for preparing for presentations by creating virtual profiles ('souls') for key attendees. These 'souls' are built from available data (e.g., transcripts, backgrounds) and used to query a knowledge graph, generating personalized questions and follow-up questions tailored to each individual's interests, thereby focusing analysis and creating relevant reports.
techniques.sourceQuote: why not to... Because... Sorry, I'm just thinking right now, you already know the people. You want to go for the presentation... why not to create an empty file and technical terms. That is actually their soul instead of a skill. A soul. Finally, let those mental models in a very simple version ask the entire database for a couple of questions and not only get the questions, but get follow-up questions based on their interests.

techniques.name: Shortening the Project View (Shortened Swim)
techniques.description: A project management strategy to combat the 'messy middle' by intentionally breaking down large, daunting tasks into much smaller, more manageable segments. This increases visibility, makes progress more apparent, and helps maintain motivation.
techniques.sourceQuote: One is to just shorten the view. Right? Shortened swim. We talk about shortened sharp like what are those bits in the middle.

techniques.name: AI-Powered Meeting Summaries and Feedback
techniques.description: Utilizing AI tools (like Claude, Krisp, or MCD) to automatically generate summaries of conversations or provide feedback on specific engagements, aiding in information retention and documentation.
techniques.sourceQuote: I will fairly routinely now you know, pop open, you know, whatever. Claude right. Which has Krisp MCP as the most common MCD that I'm using right now to be able to get. Okay. What's what happened this week? What you know, have feedback to me.

techniques.name: Accountability Partner & Time-Boxed Tasks
techniques.description: A project management approach involving regular check-ins with an accountability partner and breaking down tasks into small, estimated time increments (e.g., no more than an hour) to improve visibility, adherence, and manage the 'messy middle,' especially in new work domains.
techniques.sourceQuote: Every morning I meet with my accountability partner or whatever, and I'd say, I'm going to do these things, and I estimate how long I think something will take, and I try to break it down so that nothing takes more than an hour.

techniques.name: Task Breakdown for Predictability
techniques.description: A method to manage complex tasks by breaking them down into smaller, estimated segments, ideally none taking more than an hour, to improve visibility and avoid a 'messy middle' phase.
techniques.sourceQuote: I'm going to do these things, and I estimate how long I think something will take, and I try to break it down so that nothing takes more than an hour.

techniques.name: Multiple Choice AI Prompting
techniques.description: A technique for interacting with AI models where the user asks the AI to offer multiple choice options in its response, which helps drive the conversation forward and clarify the user's preferences, even if none of the options are perfect.
techniques.sourceQuote: I try to tell them to, especially nowadays, like to offer me multiple choice when they come back to drive the conversation forward.

techniques.name: Love the Ball Framework
techniques.description: A simplified focus strategy, derived from 'The Inner Game of Tennis,' to be employed during moments of fatigue or mental exhaustion. Instead of overthinking mechanics, the practitioner focuses on a core, fundamental element ('loving the ball' in tennis), which instinctively guides attention and body movement for optimal performance.
techniques.origin: The Inner Game of Tennis (Guy's tennis coach, referenced by Ray)
techniques.sourceQuote: So there's one thought that you can hold in your head. Let's love the ball because if you love the ball, you'll move towards it, and if you love the ball, you'll pay attention to it. If you're paying attention to it and you're moving towards it, you are far more likely to be successful than if you don't do those things.

contentAngles.angle: When DMCA strikes: The unexpected proof of real value and impact.
contentAngles.audienceHint: Creators, businesses, content strategists, legal professionals

contentAngles.angle: Lessons from the Anthropic scandal: What the Claude source code leak teaches us about AI security and open source risks.
contentAngles.audienceHint: AI developers, cybersecurity experts, tech leaders, legal counsel

contentAngles.angle: Navigating the 'Messy Middle': Strategies to push through project purgatory and maintain momentum.
contentAngles.audienceHint: Project managers, entrepreneurs, consultants, anyone leading complex projects

contentAngles.angle: The hidden work of knowledge graphs: Making invisible data analysis tangible for high-stakes presentations.
contentAngles.audienceHint: Data scientists, consultants, strategists, executive leaders

contentAngles.angle: Beyond data points: Crafting 'stakeholder souls' for hyper-personalized presentations and impactful insights.
contentAngles.audienceHint: Data analysts, sales professionals, consultants, public speakers

contentAngles.angle: The power of process over perfection: Why transparent communication about project challenges builds client trust.
contentAngles.audienceHint: Consultants, freelancers, service providers, client success managers

contentAngles.angle: Automate your way out of the 'messy middle': Small systems that deliver big consistency and reduce mental load.
contentAngles.format: Blog post, Tool review, Automation guide
contentAngles.audienceHint: Entrepreneurs, small business owners, productivity enthusiasts, tech-savvy professionals

contentAngles.angle: Personal stories of navigating the 'messy middle' in professional or personal journeys can serve as valuable 'emotional side thought leadership content'.
contentAngles.audienceHint: Professionals, entrepreneurs, anyone facing long-term projects or life transitions.

contentAngles.angle: The psychological impact of AI-driven context switching: a new cognitive muscle.
contentAngles.audienceHint: Tech professionals, knowledge workers, AI users

contentAngles.angle: Beyond motivation: Why the 'view at the peak' (end goal) is more powerful than avoiding regret in long-term challenges.
contentAngles.audienceHint: Entrepreneurs, career changers, anyone pursuing ambitious goals
